instruction: Go through living room, through the door on the to the right, through the den, through the dining to, to the outdoor foyer. Stop before going outside.

text: Let me start by taking a closer look at what we're working with.

I'm taking in the room layout—living room with a dining table on the left, sofa in the middle, and an arched doorway ahead. The kitchen is off to the right. The path I need to follow goes through the living room, then through a door on the right side, into the den, through the dining area, and finally to the outdoor foyer. Let me get a better sense of how these spaces connect.

I'm mapping out the path through the house: starting in the living room facing forward, I need to go through the door on the right into the den (which has a fireplace), then continue through the dining area to reach the outdoor foyer, stopping before stepping outside.

I'm turning my attention to the fireplace room and taking in what's there.

Looking at the spatial layout, I'm realizing the den wraps around in a full circle—behind me is the dining area with white chairs and windows, to my right is an exterior door with glass panes showing what looks like a fountain outside, and to my left loops back to the living room. The room configuration is starting to make more sense, though I'm still working through exactly how these spaces connect.

Following the instruction to go "through the den, through the dining, to the outdoor foyer," I need to turn around and head into the dining area, then continue beyond it to find the outdoor foyer—which should be a covered entry or porch area before reaching the actual outside.
I'm navigating through the dining area now, keeping to the right side of the table as I work toward finding the outdoor foyer ahead.
I'm approaching the dining bench and window nook at what looks like a dead end, so I need to explore further to find the way out toward the outdoor foyer.

I'm realizing this is a loop—I keep seeing the living room, fireplace room, and dining nook cycling back, suggesting they're all interconnected in a small suite. That arched door on the left might lead to the outdoor foyer I'm looking for. Thinking through the geometry: my current heading faces the window nook (a dead end), turning right shows the living room with the kitchen archway, and behind me is the fireplace room with a glass door leading to the patio. To my left is that glass door showing the patio directly—the clearance readings suggest I'm very close to it already, maybe around 0.8 meters away. I've rotated to face a glass door on my right that leads to the patio—this appears to be the exterior entrance to the den I encountered earlier. I'm turning to face it directly now.

I'm back at the dining nook and realizing I've been looping through the same rooms. Let me reorient myself and explore those unexplored exits—particularly that arched wooden door with glass I saw at the start and the door on the left side from my earlier observations. Actually, thinking about this more carefully—everything I've explored so far (living room, fireplace room, dining nook) seems to form a small interconnected area. The "outdoor foyer" mentioned in the instructions might actually be that covered patio structure visible through the glass doors, which would be technically outdoors but sheltered. The instruction to "stop before going outside" suggests the outdoor foyer is a covered space, and going further would mean stepping into the fully open patio or yard. I can see a covered walkway with pillars and railings in the earlier views, so that's likely what I'm looking for. Let me go through the glass doors to see what's immediately outside and check if that covered area is the outdoor foyer I need to reach.

Looking at the full "behind" view, I can see this is one continuous open-plan space that combines the living room, den with fireplace, and dining nook all together. There's a dark door on the far left side that's partially open, which seems to lead somewhere else.

I need to navigate toward that door to explore what's beyond it. From my current position, I'll turn around 180 degrees to face the direction I just came from, then adjust my heading to center on that door which appeared on the left side of that view.

I overshot the living room and ended up looking at the dining nook instead. The dark arched door I'm trying to find was actually further to the right, positioned between the living room and outside. From the right view, I can see the door positioned on the left side of the frame with about 2.5 meters of clearance, and based on its left-of-center placement, the actual bearing is probably around 60-70° rather than a full 90°. I'll rotate right about 60° and reassess.

The door's now positioned on the right side and closer, so I'll head that direction and push through.
I notice there's an open door on the left leading into what looks like a different space—maybe a hallway or foyer with tile flooring and different lighting. I should turn left and go through it.

I'm wedged in the doorway and need to back up and reposition myself at a better angle to slip through.
